skill.name: infsh-cli
skill.description: Use the inference.sh CLI to discover and run cloud AI apps for images, video, LLMs, search, 3D assets, and social automation.
skill.summary: Run inference.sh cloud AI apps from the command line.
skill.author: inference-skills

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many command, network, filesystem, and credential findings, but most backtick and weak-crypto alerts are Markdown documentation false positives. The confirmed risks are legitimate but material: pipe-to-shell installation guidance, authorized belt command execution, cloud network submission, local file uploads, and API key handling. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found.

medium_findings.title: Pipe-to-shell installer guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends running a remote installer with curl piped directly to sh. This is a supply-chain risk because users execute network-delivered shell code before local review, although the skill also documents a manual checksum-based install path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell command is directly present in install instructions. It appears to install the expected CLI, so the concern is supply-chain exposure rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Authorized external CLI execution
medium_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access for belt commands and contains many examples that run remote inference.sh apps. This is expected for the skill, but it can trigger paid cloud jobs, network calls, and account actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and command examples clearly show external command execution. The commands are scoped to belt and support the advertised purpose.

medium_findings.title: Local file upload to cloud apps
medium_findings.description: The documentation states that local paths are automatically uploaded when passed as inputs. This can expose private files if a user provides sensitive paths or follows examples without reviewing what will be sent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-upload behavior is explicitly documented with absolute, relative, and home-directory paths. This is legitimate functionality but creates clear data disclosure risk.

medium_findings.title: API key and local credential handling
medium_findings.description: The skill documents login, local credential storage, and the INFSH_API_KEY environment variable. This is normal authentication behavior, but users must protect keys and avoid exposing environment values in shared logs or prompts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references directly mention local credential storage and API key use. No evidence shows exfiltration, so the finding is limited to sensitive credential handling.

medium_findings.title: Network-dependent cloud execution
medium_findings.description: The skill routes prompts, media, and app inputs to inference.sh cloud services and third-party app providers. Users should treat all submitted inputs as data sent outside the local workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill is explicitly about cloud AI apps and links to hosted inference.sh resources. This confirms network use but not malicious network behavior.

low_findings.title: Filesystem writes for install and completions
low_findings.description: The manual install and shell completion examples write binaries or completion files into user and system configuration paths. These writes are user-directed and common for CLI setup, but they can alter shell behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are visible in documented install and completion commands. They are expected CLI setup actions, not hidden persistence.

low_findings.title: Markdown command examples flagged as Ruby backticks
low_findings.description: Most external command detections come from Markdown inline code and fenced bash examples. I did not find Ruby code execution or dynamic shell construction in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are Markdown documentation with command examples and tables. No executable Ruby source or runtime backtick evaluation was found in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe-to-shell installer guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends running a remote installer with curl piped directly to sh. This is a supply-chain risk because users execute network-delivered shell code before local review, although the skill also documents a manual checksum-based install path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell command is directly present in install instructions. It appears to install the expected CLI, so the concern is supply-chain exposure rather than confirmed malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local file upload to cloud apps
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation states that local paths are automatically uploaded when passed as inputs. This can expose private files if a user provides sensitive paths or follows examples without reviewing what will be sent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file-upload behavior is explicitly documented with absolute, relative, and home-directory paths. This is legitimate functionality but creates clear data disclosure risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: API key and local credential handling
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents login, local credential storage, and the INFSH_API_KEY environment variable. This is normal authentication behavior, but users must protect keys and avoid exposing environment values in shared logs or prompts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The references directly mention local credential storage and API key use. No evidence shows exfiltration, so the finding is limited to sensitive credential handling.

content.user_title: Run Cloud AI Apps with inference.sh CLI
content.value_statement: Teams need one command-line workflow for many hosted AI models and media tools. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use inference.sh CLI commands to find apps, run jobs, and track results.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install and authenticate the inference.sh CLI.
Lists and searches available inference.sh apps by category or query.
Runs hosted apps with inline JSON or saved input files.
Generates sample input files for supported apps.
Tracks long-running tasks and saves task results.
Explains local file upload behavior for media inputs.
limitations: Requires an inference.sh account and working belt CLI installation.
Runs jobs on external cloud services, not only on the local machine.
Local file inputs may be uploaded to cloud apps.
App availability, pricing, and schemas can change outside this skill.

use_cases.title: Prototype AI Media Workflows
use_cases.description: Find image, video, audio, and 3D apps, then run quick experiments from the terminal.

use_cases.title: Run Model Tasks from Development Tools
use_cases.description: Call LLM, search, and utility apps while working inside Claude Code or Codex.

use_cases.title: Automate Hosted AI Operations
use_cases.description: Use saved inputs, background tasks, and task status checks in repeatable command-line workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Check Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether the inference.sh CLI is installed and whether I am authenticated. Explain any missing setup steps before running apps.

prompt_templates.title: Find an App
prompt_templates.prompt: Find inference.sh apps for my task: [describe task]. Compare the best options and show the command to inspect the selected app.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Run [app name] with this goal: [goal]. Create or request the needed input values, then show how to track the task result.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Async Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a repeatable inference.sh workflow that submits a long-running media job, stores the task ID, checks status, and saves the final output.

output_examples.input: Find an image generation app for a product mockup.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies suitable image apps, recommends one, explains the input fields, and provides a terminal command to inspect the app before running it.

output_examples.input: Run a video generation task without waiting for completion.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares the run command, submits it in background mode, records the task ID, and explains how to check status later.

output_examples.input: Use a local image as input for an upscaling app.
output_examples.output: The assistant warns that the file will be uploaded, confirms the intended path, and then prepares the app run command.

best_practices: Review any command before running it, especially install and update commands.
Confirm local file paths before using apps that upload media inputs.
Use API keys through secure environment management and avoid logging them.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote installers to a shell without reviewing the source or using checksum verification.
Do not pass private local files as app inputs unless cloud upload is intended.
Do not run social posting or account actions without confirming the exact text and target account.

faq.question: What does this skill help me do?
faq.answer: It helps an AI assistant use the inference.sh CLI to discover apps, run hosted model jobs, and track results.

faq.question: Does it require an inference.sh account?
faq.answer: Yes. Authentication is required for most app runs and for account-specific commands.

faq.question: Will my files leave my machine?
faq.answer: They can. The referenced CLI documentation says local file paths are uploaded when used as app inputs.

faq.question: Can it run Claude or OpenRouter models?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes examples for OpenRouter Claude models and other hosted LLM apps.

faq.question: Can it automate Twitter or X actions?
faq.answer: Yes, but users should confirm every post, message, follow, like, or repost before execution.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium-risk warning because it uses cloud services, credentials, external commands, and local file uploads.
